speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape 6 with Walter Austerer. You-- if you could please continue where you were.

speaker: interviewee
text: I would like to tell about that one situation. We went with a-- we met a friend of ours in Vienna, a very close friend of ours, a urologist who had a conference in Vienna. We met him there and we went with him to one of the wine places, [NON-ENGLISH] outside of Vienna-- and sort of family tables. And we were sitting next to a couple, two women, mother and daughter. She was a-- the daughter was an airline hostess. And we started to talk. And they spoke English-- the daughter fluently, the mother, almost. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we engaged them in-- they engaged us in a conversation. And they insisted on driving us back to Vienna. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the mother insisted on meeting us the next day. And she would drive us to the outskirts, show us a couple of places. What I want to bring up is that it was very obvious that my wife-- assuming that I don't look that Jewish, but my wife does, and, and our friend does, maybe a little more so. And I speak Viennese fluently and, and not with any accent. So-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and, and, and she told us about-- I think she was in a film-- the mother-- in a film company during the war. Or she did some filming, or whatever she did.

speaker: interviewer
text: But never was discussed, never did she ask, what did you do? Did you-- where were you during the war? What are you doing? It was very obvious that, that we were Jewish, that we-- that I had-- that I was a refugee. But these things are just not being discussed. You don't bring it up.

speaker: interviewee
text: And, and the Austrians have never dealt with it. Germany did. But the Austrians never did. And I think it was a very, very telling incident. And I have, you know, just-- have very-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] no feelings at all for, for, for Austria at this point. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: In both of the times that you were there, did you meet anyone in Vienna or that you had known before the war? Did you meet up with anyone that you knew?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just my cousin, who had returned, and who was living here.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm. It's just about 50 years since the end of the war. A lot of-- a lot of experiences that you've-- you experienced before, during-- for-- if there were one message that you would like to leave for future generations, for yourself, for your children, what would that message be?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I think that, that the only weapon we have to, to fight the kind of evil that we experienced, the unspeakable horror and pain and, and, and loss that we all experienced-- when I say 'all,' I'm speaking for all people like myself who went through it. That the only weapon we have is-- to, to counteract it is to, to believe in life and to continue our lives as soon as we can and reconstruct our lives the best we can-- and for my family, and have children, and to be active, and be productive, and tell our story. I'm very happy that I have an opportunity to tell our story. And to, to learn to love again, to be human beings again. I think to, to be fully human is the only way to counteract that kind of horror.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're very welcome. This is my father in uniform during World War I.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the branch and rank he had in this--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't. I don't. I believe he was a corporal, but I'm not sure. This is a wedding picture of my mother and father. My mother's name was Rose-- maiden name Rose Greenfield. My father was [? Moses Max ?] Austerer. It was taken in Vienna in 1922. This is a photograph of myself. I must have been five year-- four or five years old. I guess that was the forerunner of my skiing career, also taken in Vienna. These are my father's citizenship papers, dated 1933. They were very difficult to obtain. He had been in the-- he had been in the army, had been the-- serving in the army, had been in the country living for many years. But eventually, he was able to get his papers. This in my mother's passport, which enabled her to, to eventually leave Austria. It's a German passport with the swastikas on it. And, as you can see, every Jew was given the name-- every Jewish woman was given the name Sara. And on the other side of the page is the large J to indicate that she was Jewish. This is my father's picture taken, I would estimate, around '36, maybe '37, the only picture I have of him at that-- around that period. This is the memorial to the Jews, including my father, who were, were killed in Yugoslavia. This is a mass grave in Belgrade. And the memorial was put up by the Jewish [NON-ENGLISH], a Jewish organization in Vienna. And it tells in two languages the-- what happened, that 800 people were killed in 1944, that they were murdered by the Nazis. And I visited there in '69. I, I went there. This picture of myself about a couple months after I joined the Army in 1943, United States Army. This is a picture taken on my wedding day when I married my first wife in Brooklyn in 1949. On the left is my mother, Rose, myself, my wife, Norma, her father, [? Gus, ?] and her mother, [? Lillian. ?] This is a picture of my wife, my-- Norma, my son, Steven, and my other son, Warren. I believe it was right after Stephen's bar mitzvah. And he was 13. Warren was 10 at the time. This was taken soon after I married my second wife, Sylvia. And on the left is her son, Jordan. In the middle is Warren. And on the right is Steven. Her other son, Scott, is in Florida, and he is not in that picture. This was taken in, I would say, in 1986 or 1987. This is my second wife, Sylvia. And I've been very fortunate to have been married to two wonderful women.

speaker: interviewer
text: Walter, on behalf of the Shoah Visual History Foundation, I want to sincerely thank you for making this video tape possible.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, thank you for letting me tell you my story.
